Batteries 51 Conclusion 52 Further research 52 Bibliography 54 Appendix 60 Introduction The department of  R egional  S ales in  Gasprom   Neft   LLC (GPN)  is responsible for selling motor oil. 17% of revenue (author’s calculations based on annual report) of the department   came from road transportation industry in 2019. Thus, workers in regional sales department are interested in car innovations. They are responsible for products and services that are offered to car drivers. In 2020 majority of car drivers used internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles to drive. They are fueled with petrol that GPN sells. However,  h ead of Sales Development at Regional Sales  noticed that the customer of  Gasprom   Neft  – drivers of ICE vehicles are blamed to choose ecologically unfriendly type of energy to fuel their vehicles.  The transport sector accounts for 23% of all greenhouse gas emissions in the world  (Climate Change, 2014). So, automobile manufacturers (OEMs) are appealed to divert their model offering to technological analogues.  The tendency seems alarming. The department is in danger of losing money if the technology, that is in basis of driving, changes.  One of the major alternatives to  ICE vehicles  viewed on international automobile market is electric vehicle (EV).  By definition, an “e lectric vehicles (EVs) use an electric motor for propulsion with rechargeable batteries for electricity storage. The energy in the batteries provides the motive and auxiliary power to the vehicle ”   ( Bandyopadhyay ,  2020) .   Worldwide 2,7% of i ndividuals and legal entities choose electric vehicles to  drive . In 2019 there were 7.2  million   of electric  vehicles on a road  (BNEF, 2020) .  As international practice  shows  other oil companies  such as Shell, British Petroleum, Total, Chevron find their ways to respond to the market change by tapping into fueling  of  those electric vehicles on energy distribution stage of value creation. GPN’s employees are then interested ,  what kind of strategic respond should the company pursue . This question is addressed in this paper.  The object of a research is electric vehicle market development and the subject is the commercial opportunities on domestic EV market for GPN.  The g o al of my research is to  develop recommendations for Gazprom   Neft  with a view to mitigate the threat of e-vehicles market development  I need to find a solution or a combination of solutions that are commercially attractive.  In order to design recommendations for the company I will need to follow consultancy objectives: Realize the actual threat to company’s revenue through c alculation of actual financial loss on domestic market  of fuel distribution Delineate solution options according to  market segments of electric mobility Filter strategic  options  based on decision-making of other oil counterpart (Shell) that ha s  already taken on electric transportation Review product and integration opportunities on the market of geographical choice Check the solutions against internal basis of competitiveness Conclude with chosen strategic alternatives The first chapter  has three parts: t rends on electric mobility market , s trategic  p roblem  s tatement  and industry overview. Strategic  p roblem statement   exerts the real threat to motor oil substitution of GPN and  identify  the  change in customer behavior connected with the technological change . Industry overview   provide s   possible solutions along the supply chain of electric vehicles . It includes   resources procurement for battery and cars building,  i.e.   where they are extracted, technologies for doing ;  battery technologies, electric drivetrains’  manufacturing  and producers plans ; charging infrastructure  includes  charger  segments and  types ; finally  electricity procurement  is essential for EV industry . Market lucrativeness of each supply niche  is evaluated . Having a range of commercial options to tap into, in the second chapter I look at decisions made by  another oil company,  how  it has  adopted to the change.  Simultaneous benchmarking with an oil company will  allow to  reflect it  strateg ic moves on GPN’s reality .  This insight is important to have a factual understanding of different electrification scenarios and juxtapose them on Russian conditions in the next chapter.  The third   chapter  is actual solution development. It combines internal competences’ analysis  to identify the strategic gap that GPN has in competence in  comparision  to international counterparts. Domestic competitive analysis then sets competition rules. Finally, the strategic options are provided based on specificity of  Russian  electric vehicle market development.  Installation of e lectric chargers  is  the most typical resolution met by other oil companies in response to market electrification. I will look at this solution more closely and how it may become valuable for GPN. However,  as  GPN is a vertically integrated company with vast value chain ,  commercial opportunities might be integrated with different ties of value chain, it is worth looking at different competences the company may exploit and different commercial opportunities present on the market.  There are two limitations to this paper. Firstly, t here are other technological options on automobility market such as hydrogen vehicles and different types of vehicles on gas. The head of Strategic Development perceives electric mobility  a s the most promising type, which is supported by macrotrends in valuation of number of electric vehicles to other vehicles types in future.  Secondly,  recommendations will be created  for  the Russian market. The following research papers might take other perspectives to study opportunities further on.  Electric vehicle industry worldwide and in Russia Global development trends One of the industries that consumers up to 44% of globally produced oil is road transportation. The oil industry demand is, therefore, largely relies on vehicles’ necessity in petroleum-produced products such as motor fuel and diesel. Cautiously for the oil industry, oil-fuel automobility is blamed for emissions of greenhouse gases (GHG) that have a negative impact on plant’s ecology. In 2018 Greenpeace has published a report, where manifested that the industry has a carbon footprint larger than the one of the EU. For oil establishment this public sentiment is a reason for concerns, as businesses are looking for carbon neutral technologies of transportation and regulators underpin them in that search placing favorable conditions for production and sale of environmentally neutral products as well as holding of such products for consumers.  For many carbon-neutrality infers a different technology than internal combustion engine motor.  In a range of countries, the first-priority mean to decrease carbon dioxide emissions is the development of electric transportation. Thus, a transformation is taking place in the global transport market. 9 countries had over 100 000 e-vehicles and 20 countries have reached above 1% market share. Over the five-year period (2014-2019) an average annual increase was 60% (BNEF, 2020).  Major energy agencies look into the past of electric vehicle demand and draw predictions.   BNEF in EV Outlook and OPEC in World Oil Outlook draw the most positive scenarios of production and consumption.  Bloomberg shows that in 2019 out of  85.094.954  light passenger vehicles (LPV) sales of electric cars presented not more than 3,1% ( 1.792.314 ). Same forecast predicts that by late half of 2030 EVs will beat ICEs in sales ( 48.795.799  out of  98.449.383  sale units), continuing a confident climb the market upward, whereas traditional cars reportedly inevitably keep a declining pace. There are other opinions. For instance, British Petroleum and Exxon Mobile forecast demand to be about 35 million units but not 50 million. Forecast of cars’ sales (B NEF EV Outlook , 2019) Looking at  passenger vehicle  fleet ,  the dominance of ICE will persist. An automobile park takes 15 years to change entirely (Steven, 2019, p.13). Thus, given 1,2 billion vehicles in 2020 and ICE presenting 99% of automobile park, they will hold the major share of it even when sales g e t equal. The graph depicts that by 2040 motor fuel will  still  be demanded by at least 70% of the global driving fleet. Global long-term passenger vehicle fleet by drivetrain  ( BNEF EV Outlook, 2019 ) The  steady growth of electric mobility market  is triggered by different actors. Au tomobile producers commit to electrification , for instance, Volkswagen plans to have 25% of its model line electric by 2025,  meanwhile  companies emerge whose original business model is designed for electric transportation such as American Tesla Motors and Chinese BYD. R egulatory requirements push the change in China and the EU . They alleviate taxes on e-vehicles holders and subsidies production. In other countries regulation  follow s  up the industry .   11 countries have joined EV30@30 Campaign to help the deployment of infrastructure for EVs (BNEF, 2020, p.34).  C onsumer s differ in their preference for EVs or ICE dependent on a country , but the major .  Hence, till the moment EV reaches comparable and better advantageous characteristics compared to ICE, it is governments  who push industry development.  The recent surveys show however, that in countries ,  leading the transformation, the number of drivers  who are  eager to purchase an electric model is rising dramatically annually (Figure 4).   Other market segments such as  as  taxi, carsharing,  commercial drivers   as well as  public consumer  put on alert oil companies realigning their plans for electrification .  A clear leader by  electric  sales and fleet is buses. This might  be  explained by the fact that municipalities invest in air clearance. One of the choices to combat pollution is introduction of electric public transport. Notably, this segment is leading now in electrification, but also in 20 4 0, up to 69% of them will move thanks to electricity.  Bloomberg  assumes that  light commercial  EVs and slightly behind them  passenger  EVs will climb the transition.  EV share of sales and share of fleet by product segment  ( BNEF EV Outlook, 2019 ) In Russia t here are different prediction how quickly  ele ctric  cars will penetrate the automobile market. The minimum scenario for the development of electric vehicles has already been published by the Strategy for the Development of the Automotive Industry of the Russian Federation for the period up to 2025. According to this document, the share of electric vehicles  in sales volume  should reach 5% by 2025 (129 thousand units) with the implementation of an appropriate program of support from the state. The baseline scenario envisages that their  sale  share will be 10% in 2025 (202 thousand units) and 16% in 2030 (395 thousand units).  The electric vehicle fleet is expected to reach 3% of the total vehicle volume (2 million electric vehicles). In the scenario of accelerated development, the target parameter for the share of electric vehicles by 2025 is 18% (360 thousand units), by 2030 - 30% (741 thousand units). The fleet of electric vehicles by 2030 is expected to be at the level of 5.5% of the total volume of vehicles (3.6 million electric vehicles) (Parliament of Russian Federation, 2018).  T he growth dynamics of the market volume over the past year increased by 71% in 2020, from 6 thousand EVs to  almost 11 thousand  EVs in January 2021 ( Autostat , 2021).  However, there are many factors that can accelerate or hinder market development. It will largely depend on governmental policy major discourse to adopt or not to adopt this innovation. In any case, the critical point in EV adoption on any geographic market is 3-5% of total fleet. It is a momentum when the market growth is driven by consumers rather than governmental supportive policies. In Russia, according to  federal prognosis,  this share will  reached  by 2030, lagging behind leading counterparts for about 8 years ( Sanatov   et al.,  2021). Strategic problem statement My work is based on assumption flow which I confirm or disapprove asking the client (primary data) and conducting analyses (secondary data).  Assumption 1:  Eugene, who is a strategic head at department of regional sales would like to lead an expansion of departmental operations into electric mobility market. Testing This assumption is true based on his initial request to evaluate the impact of EV market development on GPN’s revenue (evaluate the threat) and find a strategic response for a company  (i nterview , 11.01.2021) The necessity to go into electric mobility market is also proved by calculations of financial loss that a company will incur if overlooks the segment of electric  vehicles  on the market of motor oil distribution.  Petroleum products are  sold throughout Russia  (1365 filling stations),  abroad  in CIS –  Belarus, Kazakhstan, Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan  (205 filling stations), and in Eastern Europe --  Serbia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Bulgaria and Romania  (407 filling stations)  through an extensive network of directly-owned retail outlets . In Eastern Europe GPN sells petrol via its subsidiary NIS. Owing to the fact that the majority of distribution network assets are located on domestic market, I calculated the share shrinkage in Russia. How much money won’t be earned each year up to 2030 due to car park electrification in Russia and what is share of this potential unearned? The potential loss is composed of  Unspilt  liters are the result of the  The former information is presented threefold. There is my prognosis of electric cars fleet based on the growth rate of years 2019-2020 – 71%. There are also other forecasts on the number on EVs in Russian car fleet   issued in strategy of development of automotive market of Russian Federation till 2025 (Government RF, 2018). The two scenarios predicting pessimistically 2 million and optimistically 3,6 million by 2030, are both more optimistic about EV market development in Russia in comparison to my calculations. They assume 80% and 90% annual growth rates correspondently (see appendix for calculations).  All prognosis  cover  only light passenger vehicle (LPV). The annual demand for motor oil is  taken from  GPN marketing forecast (2021)  till 2035 only for LPV. T he share of  Gasprom   Neft  on the market  of filling station is 20% (GPN, 2020). To get the motor fuel price annually, I took the price inflation for petrol in Russia for the last 2016-2019 average delta and prolonged the price trend till 2030. The growth rate I got was 6,5% on average. In this forecast, given that in 2021 the price is 47,62 rub/liter, by 2025 it will grow to 62,42 rub/l and by 2030 it will be 87,5 rub/liter. Having the variables set, there are three lines, outlining potential company’s money loss on the motor fuel market. The summarized loss from that substitution in a run of 10 years was calculated to become 30.53 million rubles with current growth rate, 45,63 million rubles in balanced and 70,13 million rubles in accelerated scenarios.  Worth noting, that despite growing EV fleet, the share loss stays relatively small. Even in the most optimistic scenario of EV market development  out  602  million rubles   presumably earned  in 2030 only 5 ,8 %  (35 million rubles)  is lost . Another factor of potential financial loss is crude export. GPN exports its products internationally. The information about directions of international export is not disclosed. With that data unavailable is impossible to calculate how much will the revenue stream of crude export diminish. But as the most popular countries of Russian oil export are the Netherlands, USA, Latvia, Turkey and China it can be assumed that export will diminish. This is stated based on a fact oil demand is dependent on transportation, whereas Netherlands, USA and China are countries with leading transport electrification   trends (BNEF, 2019). In conclusion, there are basically two motivation s  for GPN to go into electric mobility market. First ly,   as a  reaction to a threat. In case, GPN does not capture the market segment of  fueling electric cars, the missed share will be enlarging within years, as we see that EV fleet is growing sharply globally as well as domestically.   The second motivation might be  expansion into electric mobility market  as it presents many market opportunities .  It can turn out to be a  market threat that  ends up  to be  an opportunity.   The positive motivation to tap into the market is it is market potential.  The example of China shows that the improvement of transport based on new energy sources (primarily electric transport) stimulates the creation of new projects and industries: for example, at the end of 2020, there were more than 180 thousand such companies in China, of which 16% were in the research and technical services sectors, and 30% was created directly in 2020. Based on this experience, it can be assumed that by 2030 in Russia there will be up to 30 thousand new enterprises associated with transport on new energy sources , and the total market for electric transport and electric mobility will amount to more than  RUB 7.5  billion  ( Sanatov , 2021).  The assumption about market growth brings us to the theory of technological innovation development. Technological innovation is organized in such a way that when an innovation has just entered the market, it is adopted by technophiles. If the innovation is successful, then – by early majority group, and this is already the stage when mass market purchases a device. From the perspective of competing firms, as the product being adopted by mass market, few players stay to compete for its production, as the majority of them has been shaken out ( Vernon ,  Heckscher- Ohli ).  As it is wisely noticed in report “Electric Vehicle Market and Charging Infrastructure Development Perspectives in Russia”, electric vehicle is not a product that may not pass the early adoption stage. In 20 19  in countries leading the development of electric mobility market  ( China, United States, Japan, Korea ) , the decline in expansion growth has been noticed .  On average in 2019 had the lowest growth delta (40%) compared to all previous years.  Experts attribute it to the fact that as govern mental support programs that helped the product to overcome early adoption stage has ended . One should equate the price of EV to ICE without subsidies in sequent 3-4 years.  A utoproducers  actively extend model line s   and  improve technology, promising a longer time till rechange and several other indicators of convenience, safety and quality  (BNEF, 2019) .   The window of opportunities for development of electric chargers in Russia is estimated to be 3-4 years ( Sanatov   et al ., 2021), till  the  EV fleet  reach es  at 3-5%  in total car fleet  (turning point)  and consumption will turn to early majority group of consumers .  Sooner or later other rivals will grasp the advantage. Lukoil and Rosneft, the biggest Russian oil companies and GPN  rivals will tap into the business. Unless they are slow to do so, international rivals, such as Shell, will diversify to Russia. To  sum up,   electric mobility  is a direct substitution to motor oil.  The client of my research is head of strategic department at regional sales of GPN. His zone of responsibility is first and foremost connected with distribution of motor oil.  But in fact ,  competition occurs not only on product distribution level. Electric vehicles are end consumers of another energy type – electricity. And electricity in a profound manner competes with oil as a fuel for transportation. Although electricity generation stands down along the value chain of electric vehicles, it is also a  competing  element. Electricity as a source of transportation brings new conditions of consumption. Patterns of consumer (drivers) behavior are embedded in social practices that users are accustomed to. A technology predetermines how a social practice is formed. A driver who possesses an internal combustion engine car or diesel (technology), charges her car at filling stations (a social practice). However, the behavior of EV drivers changes.  Drivers would come to  Gasprom   Neft  filling station to charge their cars it was a) convenient for them and b) when there were electric chargers. But so far there aren’t any electric chargers. And if there were chargers, would customers come? In an interview with a practitioner from the market of electric vehicles, co-founder of  TOUCH Electric Vehicle charging company ,  (10 .04.2021 ), it became clear that drivers of EV may avoid filling stations.  One of triggers that determine customer behavior is the time of charging. Dependent on a type charger, time varies from 15 minutes to several hours. Hence, for a driver it is no more convenient to charge their car at special charging station, because on this routine practice they would spend at least two-three time more than filling an oil tank of their cars. They then seek to make their EVs charged when doing something else, being at home, at work, at shopping malls, at gyms and so on. They start looking for particular locations where those chargers are situated to compliment  with  another activity.  This behavioral change infers that simply putting chargers at filling stations is not evidently an option. Thus, a more detail analysis is needed to find what is a solution for oil companies regarding transport electrification. The final  reason , pointed at by company’s representative (interview, 15.05.2021),  for going into mobility business is that in comparison to oil EV is an intellectual technology rather than a resource. One of megatrends is that intellectual capital but not resource endowment has become the most valuable asset in economy. Securing a share in electric mobility might create a value for the company, because EV is a tangible product.  Industry overview Further on I decompose electric vehicle industry to its elements to identify best market opportunities for  Gasprom   Neft . Each of market elements has an own supply chain. The company will need to make decisions which type of solution s  to make. So, the further section should give a big picture of options available to GPN. I want it also to shed light on critical choices inside those  electric mobility  market elements and supply chain integration options.  To understand what are the elements of electric mobility system, let’s look at a particular case of electric vehicle usage. – A driver of electric car purchases the car with contained battery. When it is time to charge his car, he drives to a charging spot and plugs in a charger. Electricity runs by the cable to charge an empty battery. Hence, the electric car fulfils its car obligations when there is a  drivetrain , a battery, a charger and electricity. These constituencies are product diversification opportunities.  Beside the market of raw materials represents a substantial ties of battery supply chain. Raw materials are produced separately from battery production, so I’d like to start with them. Raw materials It starts with raw materials, that are  Cobalt, Graphite, Lithium, Nickel, Dysprosium, Neodymium, Praseodymium and several materials like Niobium and Tin . Those materials are denoted as critical raw materials for electric vehicles (CRM4EV, 2021).  Mix   of   metals   demanded   also   depend s  on   the   types of batteries  that will be  produced in future (e.g. the development and take-up of solid state solutions).  To evaluate commercial attractiveness of those resource exploitation, I would like to bring prognoses of market demand volume, geographical distribution and technologies applied.  A   significant   amount   –   potentially   up   to   US$30-45bn – may need to be invested in mining capacity by 2025 to help satisfy demand for EVs  (Linklaters, 2019) .  There are two majorly types of technologies used to extract lithium – mining of s podumene , which  is a lithium-rich ore contained into a special type of stone called pegmatite  and processing on salt-lake brines. The former technology is highly rentable. Pegmatite contains 1-4% of lithium and recovery rate is 60-70%, which allows good profitability in mining sites. But mining itself is complicated. Mining process and facilities requires  huge  financial investments. “ The cost for their enlargement is however more affordable for it only consists in increasing excavators and trucks rate of production. Mining thus appears very advantageous as it can fit a potential growth in lithium demand. Its backwards are the environmental damages caused by pit-digging machines, plus the pollution involved in the processing chain turning excavated raw minerals into ready-to-sell lithium carbonate ” (Grosjean, 2021) .  The second technology type – processing on salt-lakes  brines , which  are water resources with high concentrations of mineral salts.  This type  is more complicated and  it may take up to 5 years till lithium becomes available for sale. But lake processing is far less dangerous for environment.  The latter technology is more expensive though. “T he value of the concentrates produced at hard-rock mines is on average US$6,250/t  lithium  lower than that of the lithium chemical products produced at brine operations. The higher-value products from brines outweigh the higher cost and result in a forecast average 2019 margin of US$5,386/t  lithium , almost twice that of their hard-rock counterparts ” (Webb, 2019) .  Lithium supply is oligopolistic in structure. Chinese  Ganfeng Lithium along with Albemarle, SQM, Tianqi Lithium, and Livent control more than 70% of the world’s lithium supply.  ( Iclodean ,   et al .,   2017 ) In contrast to other countries, where lithium is located, Chinese companies have an advantage of developed infrastructure for metal production. In that way  Ganfeng   covers a wide swath of the lithium-ion battery supply chain, including lithium resource development, refining & processing (75% of total revenue), battery manufacturing (17% of total revenue), and battery recycling & other (8% of total revenue)   (Palandrani, 2021) . It has a broad international footprint operating in  Australia, Argentina, Mexico,  China,  and Ireland . The company has long-term agreement with major OEMs and the pressing growth of EV proposes the future production of this firm will increase twice by 2025 as well as new agreements will be set.  In a nutshell, China has a huge advantage over possessing the main resource of battery production and was quick enough to establish supply chain ties with those locations where the metal is dispossessed. There other countries who seem to surf the wave of lithium demand  timely. They are South Korea, Japan. For the international market of electric vehicles, smart Asian policy implies that geopolitics of resource endowment stays in place. Despite renewable energy should cancel primary energy’s national concentration, in fact lithium endowment underpins this dependency but the weight of power has changed from oil exporters to other metals  exporters and producers . Countries with resource endowment and established production infrastructure have a competitive advantage in supply over countries  who do not .  In comparison to China several African countries, where valuable battery components are also situated, have put few investment s  into processing to leverage production at its capacity.  Nearly 50% of world cobalt reserves are in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), 58% of lithium reserves are in Chile, 80% of natural graphite reserves are in China, Brazil and Turkey, while 75% of manganese reserves are in Australia, Brazil, South Africa and Ukraine  (UNCTAD, 2021) .   Map of lithium resource availability and geostrategic impact  (Grosjean, 2021) Refining takes place in other countries than extraction, so  value added to cobalt ores by the DRC is limited to intermediate products or concentrates. Further processing  is  mostly done in refineries in Belgium, China, Finland, Norway and Zambia to obtain the end products used in rechargeable batteries as well as for other applications .  To conclude, t hose trends spur several implications for the  raw materials  market. There is a search for raw materials market right now. Hence, there is a window of opportunity for those commercial players who may have access to geographies ,  rich with necessary raw materials.  At the same time, as practice shows, raw materials endowment is not the only indicator of success. Huge value added is praised to those who are able to integrated along the supply chain. Embracing processing and refining gives up to three fourth of all financial gain  ( Ganfeng , 2021). But this integration requires vast investment to establish infrastructure and learn technology.  Lithium producers with largest market share tends to be huge vertically integrated companies.  There is search for  battery  technology that would allow to be less dependent on natural resources. Technologies  of resource exploration, mining and refining are under development. Battery production Battery represents the most important detail of e-vehicle. It costs up to 50% of final price and is the most complicated organ of a car. So far e-vehicles stay on average more expensive than ICE due to development of batteries. Though by 2025 they are expected to reach price parity with ICE motor equaling  $101 /kWh ( BNEF, 2020 ).  We see that mainly there two types of batteries, primarily made of lithium and those primarily made of nickel. In fact, both types of batteries contain elements from one another but the engineering for those two types is different. The differentiation occurs in fuel cells of batteries. From engineering perspective there is a corpus of a battery, usually made of cobalt and there is internal filling called fuel cells.  For this reason, some of elements in batteries’ structures are common and other are different. The most popular technology is lithium-ion battery mostly due to energy efficiency and increased power per mass. It is now used by major part of original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) –  Tesla, BMW, Daimler, Geely, Great Wall, Honda, Hyundai, Volkswagen, and NEVS . However, it has its disadvantageous and is considered imperfect due to reduced autonomy of driving (till recharge), high operating temperature that threatens to impede other technical characteristics and low recyclability. Thus, other developments deem to be attractive. Below is a table that describes two types of lithium (Li-Ion being the most spread one and Li-S is a developing one) and two types nickel batteries (NiMH is the older technology, whereas Na-NiCl 2  is a recent development).  An important indicator is energy density. It measures how much energy a battery contains in proportion to its weight. The unit measurement is What-hour per kilogram. When this indicator is high, it shows that a car will be running with a smaller footprint.  Among Li-ion batteries (Lithium Titanate (LTO), Lithium Cobalt Oxide (LCO), Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC) and Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP)), NMC  shows the highest energy density.  The few  other  indicators are battery weight and dimension, characteristic s  associated with charging such as memory effect and depth of discharge,  length of battery lifecycle,  temperature  when car is in motion, recycling capacity , safety in regard to thermal events and price   ( Iclodean ,   et al .,   2017 ) . ( Iclodean ,   et al .,   2017 ).  Few of established auto-makers keep battery production. And a mature trend is a division of drivetrain and battery manufacturing. The first two largest greenfield battery manufacturers  are Chinese LG Chem and CATL, Japanese Panasonic, and South Korean SK Innovation. Their production all together takes at least three fourth of the market. Many other battery producers are also Asian and primarily Chinese.   In total there are up to 40 official battery producers.  In 2017 the Li-Ion battery market was estimated 30.186,8 million USD, whereas in 2025 it is expected to be 100.433,7 million USD  (Statista, 2021) . In conclusion, the battery market is at growing cycle stage. This proved by the number of battery manufacturers  and expected threefold market growth in 7 years.   Battery technologies are the most  important  elements of electric cars and, therefore, different  authory -agencies are developing best battery variation s .  There are a number of technical characteristics that indicate technology success. In 2020 Li-ion battery NMC has the highest energy density. The price of batteries has  shrunken  80% in last 10 years and is assumed to reach a competitive price with ICE motor in 2023. Electric car drivetrain Three are two types of electric cars.  Hybrid vehicles (PHEV) possess characteristics of both an electric vehicle and an ICE. Dependent on the area it is possible to drive only on electric energy if the commuting distance is not more than 50 km (Drive Clean, 2021).  Battery electric vehicle ( BEV )  uses a battery as an accumulator of energy. They are compliant with any charging type and can be charged starting with household charger of 120-volt   ( ibid. ).   BEV range of driving is 120-500 km dependent on a model (Clean Drive, 2021).  As figures 1 and 2 showed the market share of BEV is significantly larger. IEA WEO, 2014 Electricity has several dimensions that are important when talking about charging electric vehicles.   Voltage is expressed in  V , amperage in  amp  and electric power is measured in  Wh  or  kWh . The higher any of those indicators, the faster electric current runs.  The organization of electricity typically works in such a way that the transmission of high voltage and further voltage on power poles is monopolized. It depends on a country but usually it is one grid-provider, regardless of how electricity has been  generated.Therefore , at each location where it is possible to install a charger, an installer needs to connect with local power supplier.  Charging The global electric vehicle charging station market size is projected to reach 30,758 thousand units by 2027, from an estimated 2,115 thousand units in 2020, at a CAGR of 46.6% (Research and Markets, 2021) T he company needs to understand what kind of chargers are suitable to be dispositioned on roads. One of features of charging on a highway corridor is that a car driver wants to have his car’s battery loaded as fast as possible in order she could continue her way to destination.  Time for charge has a direct connection to type of charging station (Drive Clean, 2021).  I look into different charger types and their characteristics.  Charging an e-vehicle from a regular wall outlet. Any EV has an adopter and no specific equipment is required. Level 1 charging is suitable for charging at home, work or garage. Drive Clean advises this level for hybrid vehicles (optional charging) as well as battery cars in case their daily range is moderate and charging time is long.  Level 1 charging adds about 3.5 – 6.5 miles  (5 – 9.6 km)  of driving range per hour of charging time  (ibid.) . This type seems to be incompliant with needs of drivers on highways, because they would need to wait an hour every 5 to 10 kilometers till their car is able to drive further.  The 2 level implies installment  electric vehicle supply equipment (EVSE) . 208-volt or 240-volt circuit is needed. It can be found at many public and work charging stations as well as homes. Same standard connector is needed. Depending on battery type, charger configuration and circuit capacity charger level 2 adds 14-35 miles  (23-56 km)  of driving range per hour charging of time.  This charging types is also incompliant as it will make a driver wait an hour every 23-56 kilometers of her way. Direct Current (DC) Fast Charging  is currently the fastest available fill up. The circuit of 480-volt is supplied to cars. Not every car is equipped for DC. This level chargers are usually found in shopping centers and along major travel corridors.  DC fast chargers come with a CHAdeMO, Combined Charging System (Combo or CCS) or Tesla connector system. Most DC fast chargers will have both the CHAdeMO and Combo connectors.  The charger adds 10 miles  (16 km)  of driving range per minute of charging (ibid.). Based on criteria of time this charger type is the only compliant with driver time needs. As an average electric car fully loaded drives 319 km ( Newmotion , 2021), the charge from zero to full load will take maximum 20 minutes of waiting. Thus, this type of charger should be used on highway corridors. Innovative charging types that hav e relation with grid is called V1G and V2G.  V1G is also often described as Smart Charging. With V1G, it’s possible to control the time and magnitude of charging power from the power source to the EV. V1G can be used for a multitude of applications like congestion management, frequency regulation or charging from renewables like PV. V1G is mainly interesting if the power demand (not necessarily the energy demand) becomes too high. When this is the case, one can control the power with which cars charge remotely, so that the grid will be unburdened  (Ram, 2019) . V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid) goes further than that. It makes it possible to feed the energy in the batteries of the cars back to the grid. With V2G technology, it’s possible to control the time, magnitude and direction of (dis)charging power. An electric vehicle can feed power to the home (V2H) or building (V2B), to a load (V2L) or to the grid (V2G). V2G can be used for all applications of V1G but also for unique applications such as short-term storage for renewables like PV, higher capacity for frequency regulation, as well as for off-grid applications  (Ram, 2019) . There are also several types of charging cables connected to a charger. In contrast to petrol filling stations this is noticeable, because usually it is a fuel that varies and a plugger is the same but in case with EV it is on the contrary. The electricity current is the same whereas the charger pistol varies. This fact draws an implication that an installing company has to make an investment decision on what type of charging cable will be the most demanded. The demand for one or another types of charging cable is dependent on the most popular types of EV on the area. And hence, this decision should be met regarding local EV most popular model. As the consideration of local opportunities will be explored in further  chapter  I will come back to it later on. But so far ,  I shed light on types existing types. Combination plugs (Combined Charging System) .  The CCS plug is an enhanced version of the type 2 plug, with two additional power contacts for the purposes of quick charging, and supports AC and DC charging power levels (alternating and direct current charging power levels) of up to 170 kW. In practice, the value is usually around 50 kW  (mobilityhouse.com, 2021) . CHAdeMo  plug.  This quick charging system was developed in Japan, and allows for charging capacities up to 50 kW at the appropriate public charging stations. The following manufacturers offer electric cars which are compatible with the CHAdeMO plug: BD Otomotive, Citroën, Honda, Kia, Mazda, Mitsubishi, Nissan, Peugeot, Subaru, Tesla (with adaptor) and Toyota  (ibid) . For its supercharger, Tesla uses a modified version of the type 2 Mennekes plug. This allows for the Model S to recharge to 80% within 30 minutes. Tesla offers charging to its customers for free. To date it has not been possible for other makes of car to be charged with Tesla superchargers. Public charging can be free, pay-as-you-go or subscription-based, with prices set by network p e r property owner. The  Californian industry experts  claim that the  charging  is moving toward fee structure based on kWh used (not the time to charge a car).  Users pay 40 cent per kWh at levels 3.  Nissan LEAF with a 150-mile range  (240 km)  and 40-kWh battery would cost about $1 6 .00 to fully charge (from empty to full) using DC fast charging . Generally, the expenditures for installation of one charger are evaluated at  3.65  mln   rubles . Whereas the maintenance  c ost  are  225 000  rubles .  The p ayback  is then expected in  7-10 years  ( Sanatov  et al, 2021). In conclusion, t he competition on charging market varies dependent on location. Even in countries where the EV market is the most thriving, the players are not necessarily established, because public policy is different. In China and European  Union  the policy is pushing the market to develop, stimulating demand and procuring chargers beforehand. In those locations charging companies are established. Whereas in the USA, where EV develop at globally third fast pace, has relatively few charging offering s . Shell The logic stated by oil giant that justifies its entry to electric vehicles market was articulated by vice president of Energy Solutions, Brain Davis: “ The basic strategy was we see more of our customers moving toward electric vehicles where they’re available. Conclusion From business organization perspective,  Shell  and BP  ha ve  been running a separate department that  are  responsible for e-mobility for  roughly  5 years now.  Shell’s  Energy Solution  includes a branch concerned with energy generation – electricity. Another branch takes care of new transportation means.  Whereas BP Venture outlines five strategic fields, including  advanced mobility; power and storage; carbon management; bio- and low carbon products and digital transformatio n.  Electric vehicles  is  among  several  transport initiatives  in both cases which supports an idea about the limitation of this paper on e-mobility and possible further research about transportation formats .  In regard to market elements ,  solution s  are connected with electric power system management immediately at charging spots. Some  of  acquired companies also specialize in urban infrastructural planning and help cities and municipalities to balance the utility load, to plan and deploy chargers, to connect vehicles to  infrastructure  (such as traffic light), ensuring connectivity and safety.  Generally speaking, oil companies approach the transition in a comprehensive manner, looking at  the  whole EV driver journey of decisions and supply chains before a product meets a driver. Shell leverages cooperation OEMs (Volvo trucks), to provide efficient charging solutions, which points at opportunities for effective collaborations with car-manufacturers. Moreover, t here are many solutions connected with charging. Basically, Shell has bought into all market segments of e-charging market. It deployed chargers in different locations – cities, motorways, residential areas, office areas. It serves retail customers as well business customers, for instance, fleets and businesses. It also invests in nontraditional decisions such as platform Ample that swap batteries.  BP also invests in Li-ion battery solutions directed at ultra-fast charging.  The criteria for startup acquisition deem to be choos ing  the most talented  ones  and help them grow, internalizing competence to  the big oil head companies.  Strategic alternatives The third chapter that should elaborate strategic alternatives for GPN starts with internal resource analysis. Compared with oil counterparts Shell and BP, the analysis brings organizational, technological and resources advantages of GPN and also identifies gaps on a way to development in EV market in Russia. Resour c es ’  analysis Gasprom   Neft  is among three largest Russian oil company by reserve; is in top 10 global public companies in terms of proved reserves available for the next 22 years according to SPE-PRSM standards (GPN, 2021).  The resource and competence analysis will be conducted in regard to solutions that presented in prior chapters. As shown earlier, Shell’s decisions are concentrated around electricity, charging and lubricants . Beside those BP invests in e-mobility research and batteries. Oil resource In 201 9  the company had potential reserve replacement 123% compared to the previous year .  The company keeps opening up new drilling places. In 2019 it open 3 drilling fields in Russia and discover new 48 fertile fields in regions of its current operations.  GPN claims to be the first company that started extraction operation in Russian Arctic. The company is ambitious to become a domestic and international innovative leader among  oil   companies. It views leadership in threefold target: safety, effectiveness and technologies. Technology will allow to reduce costs, improve safety, reduce waste and increase quality of logistics. Technology help to go for new types of product s . The company stays within the frames of oil industry but it looks for alternative oil types. This is worth noting, because GPN is now a company of homogenous resource in terms of energy offer. Understanding risks, the company assumes that it is able to strike a balance on changing energy market with means of “oily” diversified portfolio.  Oil as a resource has a distant  relation to any of solution mentioned above. It has potential for electricity generation but oil as a segment shows a diminishing trend. I assume two diversification options in this sense – lubricants for EV production and exploitation of oil extraction technologies for other mining activities such as lithium, graphite, rare earths.  Lubricants for EV may become a new product in  product mix of GPN-Lubricants, which lead development and sales of GPN lubricants (GPN-Lubricants, 2021).  Technological resource As I studied the several report on GPN strategic investments and ordered consulting  projects, one of tremendous GPN’s priority is technological development. Firstly, technologies are for oil extraction: development of multi-phased  deposits  and low permeable collectors, for working in ice conditions of the arti c   s helf s , etc . (Strategy 2030) .  As mentioned, permeation of oil extraction technologies is possible to other industries. One example of such replication has already appeared.   Gazprom Neft has begun implementing an investment project to develop a cutting-edge graphite-electrode plant in the Avangard Special Economic Zone (SEZ) in Omsk  (GPN, 2021) .   Target capacity at the plant is 30,000 tonnes per year, which will cover domestic steel manufacturers’ increasing demand for heavy-duty graphite electrode .   The new plant will be integrated into Gazprom Neft’s end-to-end production chain. The raw material (feedstock) for producing graphite electrodes — petroleum needle coke — will be sourced from the Omsk Refinery’s delayed coking unit (DCU). Modernisation of this complex is currently ongoing, completion of which will allow the Omsk Refinery to become the first needle-coke producer in Russia: all of this raw material being currently imported.  The future plant will be operated by Gazprom Neft subsidiary Gazpromneft-Bitumen Materials , that is headed by Anatoly  Cherner , Deputy CEO for Logistics, Processing and Sales. By the way, graphite-electrode is one of the components in EV battery. The fact that GPN already has a successful case of integration of related business into its  value  chain testif ies  positively for scaling and replicating of similar projects. Seco ndly, digital technologies  increas e  efficiency of current  business  operations  and decision-making.  GPN’s strategy is development of digitalization in the organization. It includes customer relationship management (CRM).   In 2018,  the Digital Innovation Center  created the "OP TI  24" digital platform for the sale of petroleum products in the B2B segment. The platform includes a service card for fuel and services, an online fuel flow control system and a gas station network for freight transport. For the  GPN   filling  station, a mobile application of  filling  stations is launched, which reduced the standard refueling of the vehicle  by  1.5-2 times thanks to the transition to the online fuel payment system. The application helps the client to create an order by the number of liters or for a certain amount. The driver can pay without leaving the car using payment systems.   In February 2019, the Minister of Energy of the Russian Federation Alexander Novak appreciated the company's digital projects and announced the importance of technology replication not only  in the oil industry, but also in electric power industry, thermal power, coal mining  (Wikipedia, 2021). Physical resource Electricity generation GPN does not have an electricity unit connected with electricity generation, except for one, NIS located  in  Serbia. Can the company leverage its subsidiary asset to the favor electric market development? NIS supplies petroleum to car drivers via filling stations in Eastern Europe but originally it has been  produc ing  electrical and thermal energy from traditional and renewable resources  (NIS, 2021) .   Since 2013 NIS has put into operation, on oil and gas fields, at eight locations in Serbia, mini power plants with a maximum power of 14.5 MWe.   The environmental advantage of using these mini power plants is production of electrical and thermal energy from gas, which previously has not been used due to its high content of carbon dioxide and nitrogen, or because it could not be valorised due to the lack of gas infrastructure. NIS is also developing the electricity trade, and in addition to the Serbian market, it is present in the markets of Bosnia and Herzegovina, Montenegro, Romania, Slovenia and Hungary, and trades on the border with FYR Macedonia. Apart from mini-power plants , NIS operates Thermal Electric Station “ Pancevo ” with power up to 200 MWe. The volume production is planned to be 1400  gigaW /hour.  The next  steps in the project include completion of construction works, installation of equipment and communication lines, synchronization of main equipment and commissioning of the  Pancevo . The total expected cost of the project is around 180 million euros.  However, the facility is not integrated upward along electricity supply chain. So far ,   its  primarily focus is generation, the final electricity consumers – drivers don’t interact with the company. As this paper limitation is the domestic market, the opportunity to leverage the Serbian subsidiary can be developed in further research.  Filling stations In regard to charging, GPN does not address the change on transportation market, which means that GPN does not take the transformation of transport as a serious threat right now.  To deliver value on present transportation markets, GPN sets competitive price of motor oil, it is close to other oil producers’ prices. The demand is price-sensitive in Russia and CIS  countries,  however it is not only the price that attract demand for motor fuel in countries of GPN’s presence. The NOC has extensive distribution network, while the competition on oil outlet market is concentrated. Frequently, drivers choose  Gasprom   Neft , because it is presented in convenient locations.  The network of filling stations is under control of Regional Sales department.  At end-2018 the Gazprom Neft filling station network included 1,809 outlets, 1,209 of which operate within Russia. In 2019, the company accelerated the development of its retail network, having opened 198 new filling stations under Gazpromneft and OPTI brands throughout Russia. With new filling stations launched on a franchise basis, the company has expanded the geographic coverage of its retail business to 46 regions throughout Russia.  The company’s international retail filling station network includes 195 Gazprom Neft outlets in the countries of the CIS (Belarus, Kazakhstan, Tadzhikistan and Kyrgyzstan) and 405 in South—East Europe (Bosnia—Herzegovina, Bulgaria, Romania and Serbia) under the GAZPROM and NIS Petrol brands, managed by Gazprom Neft’s Serbian subsidiary, NIS. Regions of GPN filling stations presence  (Gasprom Neft, 20 19 ) GPN has its filling stations  basically  in  two  variations.   Firstly,  there are traditional filling stations,   also called  mixed-format filling stations , where there are  a cafe and a shop during the day, and as automated stations at night .  These filling stations are expensive  as they require serving personnel. However, those service stations beside petrol sales bring lots of related sales such as coffee. In 2019 mixed-format stations brought revenue equal to petrol sales (GPN, 2020).  The other type is fully automated filling stations , which have been designed by Russian manufacturers specifically for the Gazprom Neft retail network. Investment required to launch a modular filling station is three and a half to four times less than for a conventional station.  The  advantage of such filling stations is that, given their small size, they can be installed on parking lots next  to shopping malls and housing estates.   Gazpromneft  has launched a  redesign project  of automated station , which involves transforming into digitally-enabled interactive facilities. Media screens which serve as a modern tool for interaction with customers are one of the highlights of this new format. By the end of 2020, media screens  were  installed at more than 50 flagship stations in key regions across Russia in which the network operates.  Most attractive regions , in terms of intensity of car flow s  are Moscow, Saint-Petersburg and Kazan  (internal GPN document, 2021 , interview with  Head of Sales Development at Regional Sales , 14.05.2021 ) . Partnerships  resource The department of Regional Sales has cooperation with  KamAz .  A prototype of the KAMAZ Group’s Chelnok (“Shuttle”) unmanned goods vehicle has been tested by Gazprom Neft: this Russian-made solution — a cabless, remote-controlled electric vehicle — having been evaluated outside the producer’s test facilities for the first time  (GPN, 2020) .   The Chelnok’s  autonomous operation is facilitated by special body-mounted sensors — light-radars and computer-vision cameras. Artificial intelligence (AI) means the electric vehicle can move along a road independently and on the same basis as other road-users (including pedestrians) without posing any risk to those around it.   Deploying this unmanned vehicle will increase inventory turnover, speeding up operations at production facilities, as well as cutting fuel and other costs, as a result. “In testing and fine-tuning prototypes  of  unmanned vehicles, together with our partners, we will not only be able to scale-up successful projects throughout Gazprom Neft facilities, but, in the near future, also offer effective, user-ready solutions to the entire market , ” - says Dmitry  Potapov , general director at  Gasprom-Snabzhenie  (2020). Another valuable partnership is G PN’s  integ rity with educational institutions.   One of such partnership is participation in  EnergyHUB , a startup contest in field of energy that takes place annually in Saint-Petersburg. In 2021 the contest was won by  Powerplace   –  an intelligent power module for fast charging stations for electric vehicles. It was developed by a team of ITMO and LETI alumni. As a result of the competition, the startup will receive funding from Gazprom  Neft  and the community of energy clubs of St. Petersburg universities  (GPN, 2021). Attention of the company to innovative startups signals its readiness to embrace partnership in electric mobility. Lacking competence In comparison to Shell and BP there is no consolidated organ in GPN, that would be responsible for electric mobility. In both companies’ cases e-mobility were parts of bigger departments that aggregate decisions regarding mobility innovation and renewable energy. In GPN initiatives will arise but so  far  they belong to different departments, there is no comprehensive concentrated strategic effort that would unite solutions under a one strategic sight that grasp commercial opportunities. Beside organizational unity, GPN has not yet started competence acquisition. As shown in best international practices, oil companies buy other companies that specialize in different areas of electric mobility, generally making focus on charging accessibility and speed. Conclusion From the standpoint of oil companies, competition has not started on the market of electric mobility in Russia yet. As the electric vehicles market is at very early development stage and the turning point is consumption is not reached, there is seemingly no urgency in leveraging resources for a competition on Russian electric mobility market. However, as noted electric fleet is growing and experts predict the turning point to be reached in 3-4 years from 2021. In this sense, a more intense competition will come the same time. Thus, it is worth preparing for upcoming demand.  In regard to access to electricity market, Rosneft and Lukoil have advantages over GPN on domestic market. Lukoil has electric power and capacity installed, whereas Rosneft has tight cooperation with electricity establishment in Russia.  It then  will  be easier to those companies to offer electricity as a product, because they can leverage it as an asset.  In terms of filling station, the situation is not the most favorable either. By asset number Rosneft and Lukoil have more spots to install electric charger. Shell network of filling outlets is far less developed, but it has a rich knowledge advantage of deploying infrastructure in other locations, where electric mobility has already become a second or even first norm. Lubricants as a business is a developed direction for all players starting from production to distribution. However, it’s only Shell that has created e-fluids and e-grease especially for e-vehicles. From the perspective of innovation and mindset, Shell has a great advantage to attract international expertise and capital. Something which Russian oil companies cannot afford   owing to an important political reason. GPN is among Russian companies that are under sanctions of attracting long-term financing   from   European Union and the USA . It is also limited in cooperation with western partners on complicated drilling in Russia  (Forbes Russia, 2014) .  The positive aspect is that Russian oil establishment is resistant to perceive innovation on transportation market as a threat. As corporate mindset is longest institute to change, there is a positive window to change faster than counterparts provided GNP’s digitalization  reform and innovative collaborations with  KamAZ ,  EnergoHUB  and others. Electricity  and organizational change Electricity as a business diversification decision is ambiguous. On the one hand, in conditions of developed electric market, supplying own electricity is an additional asset that will add value to customer and will captured by the company.  Competitors on the Russian market have better access to electricity supply than GPN. At the same time   electric mobility market experts in Russia claim that there will be no need for additional energy generation in Russia, as the current market abundance that is exported to Baltic countries will be rejected starting from 2025  ( Sanatov  et al. , 2021) . The leftover will be sufficient to cover energy demand created by electric vehicles. As calculations of Germany and India show, the real burden electric vehicles will create on power system is overestimated. Indeed, electricity consumption will grow in Germany just by 4% by 2050. In India, where EV fleet is expected to be 25-30% from general, the electricity demand will grow only 2-3% (ibid.).  According to international practice of Shell and BP, both of those companies develop electricity generating and supplying facilities. For them diversification to electricity is an essential strategic choice of a bigger agenda related to decarbonization of energy sector. In fact ,  financially international players Shell, BP, Chevron experienced financial losses associated with rejection of traditional fuels, which makes them diversify business portfolio to electricity, the fastest growing energy sector. And according to ecological agenda is the only energy to which  popular renewables contribute.  Energy Solutions at Shell and BP Venture become overarching departments that supervise synergy between mobility and alternative energy sources. In Russia the e lectricity  market  was demonopolized in   p eriod of the last 20 years.   The market opportunities have opened up in generation and supply.  Most of the generating assets are concentrated in 6 wholesale thermal generation companies (WGCs)   ( Palamarchuk , 2016)  The supply sector is represented by independent retailers. The independent companies compete in prices with other suppliers for the right to provide the retail customers with electricity and capacity. Supply tariffs of the independent retailers are unregulated.  (ibid.) Finalizing ,   GPN should start its expansion into electricity sector. It would be suggested to do so in a strategic and organizational correspondence with other green initiatives in a company to fix synergies.  2  Option.  Charging The projection on the number of electric charger s  till 2030 predicts that there will be 240  charger  by 224 and 1500 chargers by 2030. How much of this market GPN can grasp? Projection on a number of electric chargers in Russia ( Sanatov  et al., 2021) By calculation of Dmitry  Satanov  [et. al] (2021) in Russia to provide sufficient infrastructure for development of electric vehicles, it is necessary to install a charger every 300 km by 2024 and every 100 km by 2030. This frequency is conditioned by average range an EV currently goes –  about 200 km. This limitation, according to technological trends, will decrease, but at the moment this factor must be overcome with the help of infrastructural solutions, balancing the distribution of the network along key communication routes (federal highways). Experts consider it appropriate to start with an average car that is oriented at 200 km on a full charge and has 30 percent battery wear. In this case   ideal conditions  are  when the distance to the nearest  charger  is about 140 km.  It  is  also  necessary to take into account a number of additional factors, including an increase in electricity consumption in the cold season, as well as behavioral  aspects: for example, a  charger  can be skipped by the driver when the charge level is high, but insufficient to overcome the path to the next gas station.  Authors  accept the estimated level of these parameters - 30%. Thus, for guaranteed uninterrupted traffic throughout the country, the maximum distance between the two nearest charging stations should not exceed 100 km . To make an own evaluation on needed density of electric chargers on highways, it is necessary to understand what models will be present in Russia in nearest future. Now  83% of all registered e-vehicles belong to the model Nissan Leaf. Other 6% of electric vehicles belong to Tesla models and 4% to Mitsubishi, model  i-MiEV . A solution more long-term and comprehensive solution is diversification of charging option portfolio.  As we see at experience of international oil counterparts, Shell has been buying already established companies with deployed network of charging stations ,  startups that are developing charging technologies  and supportive IT solution. According to market analysis by  Sanatov  [et al.] there is a lack of investment in battery technology development and production (2021).  The Russian Federation has sufficient competence to design traction batteries for electric vehicles, but at the same time, there is no production of lithium-ion battery cells with the required specific energy and in sufficient volume.  Experts assume that the  priority should be given to the development and production of batteries based on the most advanced cathode materials (NMC and LFP). Investing in batteries is not a solution that brings revenue in short term, because in Russia battery technological development is in its infancy. However, as stated in EV market overview batteries comprise up to 50% of expenditures and, therefore, the technology will bring the largest return on investment in consequent years. Conclusion This paper was looking at different commercial opportunities from the point of view of a Russian vertically integrated oil company  Gasprom   Neft .  The paper is built in a manner of narrowing down the options and providing factual recommendations. It starts with reviewing whole EV industry and investment opportunities in different element of EV market, then narrow solutions down by looking at best international practices and finally views factual conditions of competition on domestic market, provides recommendations. The  paper stresses three decisions for GPN on the market of electric vehicles: integration into electricity sector and synergizing this business unit with other green strategic initiatives for creation of an ecosystem within the company.  The second solution is connected to development of charging infrastructure. The short-term immediate solution is installation of 22 charger s  on highways M10, M11, M7 (Moscow - Kazan), as those regions were pointed at by company’s representatives as the most effective from the point of view of car flow. Longer-term solutions  may include acquisitions and partnerships with startups that develop charging technologies. Moreover, investing into battery technologies deem to be a vital long-term investment opportunity. Further research There are several directions for further research. Firstly, the recommendations concerning electricity, charging and batteries can be studies further in regard to particular investment’s option (startup acquisitions, partnerships, etc.).  Moreover, there are more  solutions that this paper did not consider, because the final recommendations followed the best international practices, but GPN could consider extraction of raw materials for electric vehicles in Russia.  Experts note that the Russian lithium resource base is one of the largest in the world, the metal is found in 16 deposits  ( Sanatov , 2021) . Lithium resources in Russia are estimated at 1-1.5 million tons, the country is in 10th place in the world. At the same time, lithium is not mined for the Russian domestic market. Instead, up to 1,500 tons of metal are imported. According to today's estimates, the raw material reserves of the Arctic contain about 98% of the reserves of nickel, 66% of rare earth metals, but their extraction, given the “fragility” of the Arctic ecosystems, is capable of upsetting the ecological balance of the territory.   Another direction for research is innovative charging options such as V1G and V2G. Those more advanced charging technologies imply developed communication between grid and a car, which require a lot of software technologies. Hence, software innovations for e-mobility can become an option for consideration.  Finally, GPN can look for other geographical areas rather then domestic market.
